1.synthesis:
Judges 13:1 opens the Samson narrative with the cycle's formulaic notation at its most compressed: 'And the people of Israel again did what was evil in the sight of YHWH, so YHWH gave them into the hand of the Philistines for forty years.' The forty-year Philistine oppression is the longest single period of servitude in the judge-cycle — longer than any of the previous oppressions (Mesopotamia 8 years, Moab 18 years, Canaan 20 years, Midian 7 years). The Philistine domination also differs from previous oppressions in one crucial respect: there is no recorded cry to YHWH. Earlier cycles begin 'and the people of Israel cried out to YHWH'; the Samson narrative begins without that cry. Israel has apparently settled into its Philistine subservience without recognizing it as a problem requiring deliverance.

This single-verse pericope functions as the theological heading for the entire Samson cycle (chapters 13–16): the oppression context within which Samson is raised and operates. The Philistines are not merely a military threat but a cultural force — and the Samson narrative will show Israel's deepest judge deeply compromised by the very culture whose domination Israel should be resisting. Samson neither leads a military campaign against the Philistines nor commands Israel's warriors — he operates alone, entangled in personal relationships with Philistine women, and his 'deliverance' is more personal vengeance than national liberation.
1.historical_context: The forty-year Philistine oppression spans the period from roughly 1100 to 1060 BCE in traditional chronologies, overlapping with the late judges period and early Samuel period (1 Samuel 4 notes the ark's capture during the same Philistine dominance). The Philistines, settled in the five-city coastal league since the Sea Peoples migrations circa 1200 BCE, possessed iron technology and military organization that Israel's tribal confederation could not match. The absence of a communal cry to YHWH in the Samson introduction reflects the book's deepening portrait of Israel's spiritual deterioration — by this point, the people no longer recognize oppression as oppression, or do not believe YHWH will deliver them.
1.literary_structure: The single-verse pericope is the thematic header for the Samson cycle. Chapters 13–16 are structurally unified by the Philistine-oppression context; within that frame, the annunciation (ch. 13) establishes Samson's divine vocation, and chapters 14–16 trace how that vocation is pursued and ultimately fulfilled in ways that simultaneously validate and challenge the reader's expectations of a deliverer.
1.theological_application: The absence of a communal cry for deliverance in the Samson narrative is the judge-cycle's most sobering spiritual indicator. Previous generations cried out; this generation has apparently made peace with its oppression — perhaps benefiting economically from Philistine trade, perhaps accommodating culturally (as Samson's marriages and relationships demonstrate). The permanent application is that the covenant community's capacity to recognize spiritual oppression depends on maintaining the contrast between covenant freedom and cultural captivity. When the community loses that contrast — when life under oppression feels normal — it ceases to cry out, which means it ceases to receive deliverance.
1.christology: The forty-year Philistine oppression without a communal cry is the Judges narrative's darkest spiritual portrait, and it prepares for the most unusual of the major judges: a deliverer who operates in the midst of the enemy culture rather than against it from outside. The pattern of YHWH raising a deliverer before Israel consciously cries out anticipates the incarnation's logic in Romans 5:8: 'God shows his love for us in that while we were still sinners, Christ died for us.' YHWH does not wait for Israel to recognize its need and cry out — he sends a deliverer anyway, from within the compromised situation, to work salvation through unexpected means.

2.synthesis:
Judges 13:2–14 narrates the annunciation of Samson's birth to his mother — a nameless woman from the tribe of Dan married to Manoah, who was barren. The angel of YHWH appears to her: 'Behold, you are barren and have not borne children, but you shall conceive and bear a son. Therefore be careful and drink no wine or strong drink, and eat nothing unclean, for behold, you shall conceive and bear a son. No razor shall come upon his head, for the child shall be a Nazirite to God from the womb, and he shall begin to save Israel from the hand of the Philistines' (vv. 3–5). The annunciation pattern — barren woman, divine messenger, miraculous conception, specific vocation announced — is the Bible's recurring birth-narrative form for those whose lives will reshape the covenant story.

Three Nazirite restrictions are specified: no wine or strong drink, no unclean food, no razor. The Nazirite vow (from Numbers 6) was normally a temporary voluntary commitment; Samson's is lifelong and divinely imposed from birth. The mother's restrictions during pregnancy suggest that the Nazirite consecration begins before birth — the entire life is dedicated to YHWH. When Manoah asks for the angel to return with instructions, the angel appears again and repeats the dietary restrictions on the mother. The one thing Manoah asks — 'What is the child's manner of life to be?' (v. 12) — is notably not answered in the same terms as the annunciation; the angel focuses on the mother's preparation rather than the child's destiny.
2.historical_context: The barren-woman-to-miraculous-birth pattern appears with Sarah (Genesis 18:11), Rebekah (Genesis 25:21), Rachel (Genesis 29:31), Hannah (1 Samuel 1:5), and Elizabeth (Luke 1:7). The Nazirite vow of Numbers 6 specified abstinence from wine and grape products, from unclean food, and from cutting the hair for the duration of the vow. The angel of YHWH is identified later as YHWH himself appearing in human form — the same messenger who appears to Gideon (Judges 6:11–22) and Abraham (Genesis 18). Samson's tribe, Dan, had already partially migrated north (ch. 18); the Dan of the Samson narrative is the original southern allocation contested with the Philistines.
2.literary_structure: The annunciation unit moves through: the initial appearance to the mother (vv. 2–7), Manoah's prayer for a second appearance (v. 8), the second appearance and instruction (vv. 9–14). The repetition of the dietary restrictions in the second appearance emphasizes that the preparation precedes the child — the mother's consecration is the first expression of the son's Nazirite life.
2.theological_application: The annunciation to Manoah's wife establishes the pattern that runs through the entire Samson cycle: divine initiative precedes human response. Samson did not choose his vocation; it was assigned to him before his birth. The covenant community member who feels trapped in a situation not of their choosing — born into circumstances of spiritual difficulty, surrounded by cultural compromise — is addressed by the Samson annunciation: YHWH can consecrate a deliverer within and for exactly those circumstances. The vocation to 'begin to save Israel' from within the compromised Philistine context is YHWH's answer to a situation that Israel itself had stopped recognizing as needing rescue.
2.christology: The annunciation to Manoah's nameless wife is one of the OT's clearest anticipations of the Lucan annunciation. Both announcements are to women who face biological impossibility (barrenness; virginity); both announce a son with a specific divine vocation; both involve dietary and ritual specification for the preparation period. The angel's response to Manoah's question — 'What is the child's manner of life to be?' — is not fully answered here, just as the full scope of the incarnation was not revealed at the Lucan annunciation. Luke 1:31–33 announces a son who will 'save his people from their sins' (Matthew 1:21) — the ultimate fulfilment of the Samson pattern of a divinely-appointed deliverer sent to rescue a people who did not know they needed rescuing.

15.synthesis:
Judges 13:15–25 narrates Manoah's encounter with the angel of YHWH and the miraculous sign that accompanies the offering. Manoah attempts to offer hospitality — 'Please let us detain you and prepare a young goat for you' (v. 15) — and the angel redirects him to offer it to YHWH rather than as a meal. Manoah then asks the angel's name so that he can honour him 'when your words come true' (v. 17); the angel replies: 'Why do you ask my name, seeing it is wonderful (peli'i)?' (v. 18). When Manoah brings a grain offering and the kid, the flame from the altar ascends to heaven — and the angel of YHWH ascends in the flame (vv. 19–20). Manoah and his wife fall on their faces. Manoah's reaction: 'We shall surely die, for we have seen God' (v. 22). His wife's reply is the passage's practical wisdom: 'If YHWH had meant to kill us, he would not have accepted a burnt offering and a grain offering at our hands, or shown us all these things, or now announced to us such things as these' (v. 23).

The chapter closes with the birth of Samson: 'And the woman bore a son and called his name Samson. And the young man grew, and YHWH blessed him. And the Spirit of YHWH began to stir him in Mahaneh-dan, between Zorah and Eshtaol' (vv. 24–25). The Spirit's stirring precedes any specific act — it is the internal preparation of the Nazirite deliverer for the work that chapters 14–16 will narrate.
15.historical_context: The angel's name — peli'i, 'wonderful' or 'beyond comprehension' — is cognate with pele', the word used in Isaiah 9:6 for the messianic child's name: 'Wonderful Counselor.' The divine name's unknowability is a consistent OT theme: YHWH's name at the burning bush is 'I AM WHO I AM' (Exodus 3:14); Jacob at the Jabbok asks the divine wrestler's name and is refused (Genesis 32:29). The flame-ascent with the offering echoes Gideon's theophany (Judges 6:21), where fire from the rock consumed the offering. Manoah's wife's theological reasoning — 'if YHWH had meant to kill us, he would not have accepted our offering' — is sound Deuteronomic logic: YHWH's acceptance of worship is a sign of continued covenant relationship.
15.literary_structure: The unit moves from the hospitality-redirect (vv. 15–18) to the theophanic sign (vv. 19–21) to Manoah's fear-and-his-wife's-wisdom (vv. 22–23) to Samson's birth and Spirit-stirring (vv. 24–25). The wife is consistently the theologically sharper of the two parents — she receives the annunciation, she corrects Manoah's fear, she is named in neither case. The unnamed woman's theological insight frames the chapter.
15.theological_application: Manoah's wife's reasoning — 'if he had meant to kill us, he would not have accepted our offering' — is the OT's practical theology of divine presence. Theophanic encounter produces fear; the question is whether that fear resolves into paralysis or into a reading of the encounter's meaning. The wife reads the evidence correctly: YHWH's acceptance of worship is a covenantal assurance of continued relationship, not a prelude to judgment. The permanent application is that the covenant community's experience of divine nearness — however awesome — is interpreted through the lens of what YHWH has already done: 'he accepted our offering' means 'he is for us.'
15.christology: The angel's name — peli'i, wonderful — anticipates Isaiah 9:6's messianic name 'Wonderful Counselor' (pele' yo'ets). The divine name that exceeds comprehension in Judges 13 is disclosed in the incarnation: John 1:18, 'No one has ever seen God; the only God, who is at the Father's side, he has made him known.' The divine name that the angel withheld from Manoah is the name that Jesus reveals — 'I and the Father are one' (John 10:30). The ascent of the angel in the flame (v. 20) prefigures the ascension: the divine messenger who came down to announce redemption ascends when his announcement-work is complete, just as Christ ascended after completing the greater work the annunciation pointed toward.
